name: Superoptimisation
summary: Reframes the optimiser as a search problem: given a short instruction sequence, find an equivalent sequence that's shorter or faster, without a fixed set of rewrite rules. Slow (minutes-hours per sequence) but transformative as a *source of optimiser rules* — Souper has contributed dozens of LLVM peephole rules.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Superoptimisation rethinks the optimiser as a search problem: given a short instruction sequence, find an equivalent sequence that is shorter or faster, *without* a fixed set of rewrite rules. Where conventional optimisation applies a curated catalogue of transformations, superoptimisation searches the entire instruction-sequence space.

The practical upshot is limited: superoptimisation is slow (minutes to hours per sequence) and only applies to short code fragments. But as a *source of optimiser rules*, it is transformative — rules proven by a solver are strictly better than rules hand-designed against test cases.

sections.title: Denali and STOKE
sections.content:
**Denali** (Joshi, Nelson, Randall, 2002) used an E-graph and SAT solver to search for instruction-level optima on Alpha assembly. The E-graph represents many equivalent forms of the input; the SAT solver searches for an extraction (one form per node) minimising a cost function.

**STOKE** (Schkufza, Churchill, Aiken, ASPLOS 2013) uses MCMC-style stochastic search over the instruction space, with a cost function combining correctness (via test equivalence) and performance (via measured throughput). STOKE proposes random instruction-sequence mutations, accepts mutations that improve the cost, and rejects mutations that fail correctness checks. STOKE found sequences for `popcount` and related primitives that beat hand-written Intel reference code — a striking demonstration that even hand-tuned assembly can be improved by stochastic search.

sections.title: Souper — proof-driven LLVM rules
sections.content:
**Souper** (Regehr, Sasnauskas, et al.) targets LLVM IR. It extracts "interesting" IR fragments from real programs, asks an SMT solver whether a shorter equivalent exists, and produces proven-correct peephole rules that can be fed back into LLVM's `InstCombine` pass.

Many real LLVM optimisations have come directly from Souper output. The workflow: run Souper over a large corpus of LLVM IR (open-source projects, benchmarks), collect the optimisations Souper finds, manually review and integrate the most impactful ones into `InstCombine`. This is a hybrid of automated discovery and human curation: Souper finds candidates the SMT solver proves correct, and humans decide which are worth shipping.

The lesson: superoptimisation's value is *off-line*. Use it to discover optimiser rules; don't try to use it as a runtime pass.
